From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Collection of audio recordings

This article is about albums of recorded sound. For other uses, see [Album (disambiguation)](/wiki/Album_(disambiguation) "Album (disambiguation)").

"Music Album" redirects here; not to be confused with [Music Album (TV series)](/wiki/Music_Album_(TV_series) "Music Album (TV series)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Compact_disc_album.jpg/250px-Compact_disc_album.jpg)](/wiki/File:Compact_disc_album.jpg)

Albums c. 2000 came on [compact discs](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") stored in [jewel cases](/wiki/Jewel_case "Jewel case").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Earlyalbum1.jpg/250px-Earlyalbum1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Earlyalbum1.jpg)

Early record albums from the first half of the 20th century resembled [photo albums](/wiki/Photo_album "Photo album"), being packaged in book form on multiple [78 rpm records](/wiki/78_rpm_record "78 rpm record").

An **album** is a collection of audio recordings (e.g., [music](/wiki/Music "Music")) issued on a medium such as [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") (CD), [vinyl](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") (record), or [audio tape](/wiki/Audio_tape "Audio tape") (like [8-track](/wiki/8-track_cartridge "8-track cartridge") or [cassette](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape")), or in [digital](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") format.

The album was the dominant format or unit of [recorded music](/wiki/Recorded_music "Recorded music") expression and consumption from the mid-1960s through to the early 21st century, a period that has been described in the industry as the *[album era](/wiki/Album_era "Album era")*.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Vinyl LPs are still issued for certain album releases, though [album sales](/wiki/Album_sales "Album sales") in the 21st-century have been predominantly in CD and then downloadable (e.g. [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3")) and streaming digital formats. The [8-track tape](/wiki/8-track_tape "8-track tape") was the first tape format, widely used alongside vinyl, from 1965 until being phased out by 1983—it was gradually supplanted by the cassette tape over the 1970s and early 1980s. The popularity of the cassette reached its peak during the late 1980s before declining sharply during the 1990s as it yielded market share to compact disc formats. In the early 2000s, sales of CDs—which also typically contained around 45-60 minutes (or 'an album') of music—became the primary distribution mechanism for recorded music, until the rise in the 2010s of digital, whereby albums could be downloaded and then streamed through 'streaming services' or 'platforms' such as [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"). An enormous variety of albums are now more widely (often globally) available, and listening to an album has been made cheaper, more convenient and more popular, through such [services](/wiki/List_of_streaming_media_services "List of streaming media services").

Most albums are recorded in a recording [studio](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) making them *studio albums*, although they may also be recorded at a [concert venue](/wiki/Concert_venue "Concert venue"), at home, in the field, or in a mix of these and other places. The total time to record an album varies between a few hours and several years. Recording usually requires several [takes](/wiki/Take "Take") of each [track](/wiki/Album_track "Album track"), each component piece of the album: in each track, different parts may be recorded separately, and then brought or "[mixed](/wiki/Audio_mixing_(recorded_music) "Audio mixing (recorded music)")" together. Recordings that are done in one take without [overdubbing](/wiki/Overdubbing "Overdubbing") are termed "live", even when done in a studio. Studios are built to absorb sound, eliminating [reverberation](/wiki/Reverberation "Reverberation"), to assist in mixing different takes; other locations, such as concert venues and some "live rooms", have more reverberation, which creates a "live" sound.[[3]](#cite_note-Philip_Newell_169–170-3) Recordings, including live, when published may contain editing, sound effects, voice adjustments, and other manipulations of the sound that was initially recorded. With modern recording technology, including [multitrack recording](/wiki/Multitrack_recording "Multitrack recording"), various artists collaborating on a track or album can be recorded in separate rooms or at separate times, perhaps while listening through headphones to certain other parts recorded earlier.

[Album covers](/wiki/Album_cover "Album cover") and [liner notes](/wiki/Liner_notes "Liner notes") may accompany an album, the latter of these conveying additional information, such as song lyrics, biographical information on artists, analysis of the recording, or [librettos](/wiki/Libretto "Libretto") in the case of classical music and opera recordings.[[4]](#cite_note-Album_Cover_Art_Series-4)[[5]](#cite_note-The_history_of_the_CD_-_The_'Jewel_Case'-5)

Historically, the term "album" was applied to a collection of various items housed in a [book](/wiki/Book "Book") or photo album format. In musical usage, the word was used for collections of short pieces of [printed music](/wiki/Printed_music "Printed music") from the early nineteenth century.[[6]](#cite_note-Mendelssohn_And_Schumann-6) 'Albums' of recorded sounds were first developed for recorded music in the early 20th century: individual [78 rpm records](/wiki/78_rpm_record "78 rpm record") (78s), each side of which could contain some 3 minutes of sound (enough for a song, piece, or 'track') were physically collected together and sold in a bound book resembling a [photo album](/wiki/Photo_album "Photo album"). This format evolved after 1948 into vinyl [long-playing (LP) records](/wiki/LP_record "LP record") played at 33+1⁄3 [rpm](/wiki/Rpm "Rpm"); these contained some 22 minutes of sound per side, giving an approximate 45 minutes of music per LP record. When these LPs were introduced, a collection of pieces or songs on a single record was called an "album"; the word was extended to other recording media such as compact disc, [MiniDisc](/wiki/MiniDisc "MiniDisc"), compact audio cassette, 8-track tape and digital albums as they were introduced.[[7]](#cite_note-About_Vinyl_Records-7) An album would typically have more tracks than an [extended play](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play") (EP) record, which might contain only a 'single' (a single track released on its own), or two or three songs released together.

## History

An *[album](/wiki/Album_(Ancient_Rome) "Album (Ancient Rome)")* (Latin *albus*, white), in ancient Rome, was a board chalked or painted white, on which decrees, edicts, and other public notices were inscribed in black. It was from this that in medieval and modern times, album came to denote a book of blank pages in which verses, autographs, sketches, photographs and the like are collected.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In the early nineteenth century, "album" was occasionally used in the titles of some classical music sets, such as [Robert Schumann](/wiki/Robert_Schumann "Robert Schumann")'s *[Album for the Young](/wiki/Album_for_the_Young "Album for the Young"),* Op 68, a set of 43 short pieces.[[6]](#cite_note-Mendelssohn_And_Schumann-6)

With the advent of 78 rpm records in the early 1900s, the typical 10-inch disc could hold only about three minutes of sound per side, so almost all popular recordings were limited to around three minutes in length.[[9]](#cite_note-WRD-20140711-9) Classical-music and spoken-word items generally were released on the longer 12-inch 78s, playing around 4–5 minutes per side. For example, in 1924, [George Gershwin](/wiki/George_Gershwin "George Gershwin") recorded a drastically shortened version of his new seventeen-minute composition *[Rhapsody in Blue](/wiki/Rhapsody_in_Blue "Rhapsody in Blue")* with [Paul Whiteman](/wiki/Paul_Whiteman "Paul Whiteman") and His Orchestra. The recording was issued on both sides of a single record, Victor 55225 and ran for 8m 59s.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

By about 1910, bound collections of empty sleeves with a [paperboard](/wiki/Paperboard "Paperboard") or [leather](/wiki/Leather "Leather") cover, similar to a photograph album, were sold as record albums that customers could use to store their records (the term "record album" was printed on some covers). These albums came in both 10-inch and 12-inch sizes. The covers of these bound books were wider and taller than the records inside, allowing the record album to be placed on a shelf upright, like a book, suspending the fragile records above the shelf and protecting them. In the 1930s, record companies began issuing collections of 78s by one performer or of one type of music in specially assembled albums, typically with artwork on the front cover and liner notes on the back or inside cover. Most albums included three or four records, with two sides each, making six or eight compositions per album.[[11]](#cite_note-alancross.ca-11)

By the mid-1930s, record companies had adopted the album format for classical music selections that were longer than the roughly eight minutes that fit on both sides of a classical 12" 78 rpm record. Initially the covers were plain, with the name of the selection and performer in small type. In 1938, Columbia Records hired the first graphic designer in the business to design covers, others soon followed and colorful album covers became an important selling feature.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

When Columbia introduced the Long Playing record format in 1948, it was natural the term album would continue. Columbia expected that the record size distinction in 78s would continue, with classical music on 12" records and popular music on 10" records, and singles on 78s. Columbia's first popular 10" LP in fact was Frank Sinatra's first album, the four-record eight-song *[The Voice of Frank Sinatra](/wiki/The_Voice_of_Frank_Sinatra "The Voice of Frank Sinatra")*, originally issued in 1946.[[13]](#cite_note-Granata-13)

RCA's introduction of the smaller 45 rpm format later in 1948 disrupted Columbia's expectations. By the mid-1950s, 45s dominated the singles market and 12" LPs dominated the album market and both 78s and 10" LPs were discontinued. In the 1950s albums of popular music were also issued on 45s, sold in small heavy paper-covered "gate-fold" albums with multiple discs in sleeves or in sleeves in small boxes. This format disappeared around 1960. Sinatra's "The Voice" was issued in 1952 on two extended play 45s, with two songs on each side, in both packagings.[[13]](#cite_note-Granata-13)

The 10-inch and 12-inch [LP record](/wiki/LP_record "LP record") (long play), or 33+1⁄3 [rpm](/wiki/Rpm "Rpm") microgroove [vinyl](/wiki/Polyvinyl_chloride "Polyvinyl chloride") record, is a [gramophone record](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record") format introduced by [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") in 1948.[[14]](#cite_note-auto-14) A single LP record often had the same or similar number of tunes as a typical album of 78s, and it was adopted by the record industry as a standard format for the "album".[[11]](#cite_note-alancross.ca-11)

The term "album" was extended to other recording media such as [8-track tape](/wiki/8-track_tape "8-track tape"), [cassette tape](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape"), [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc"), [MiniDisc](/wiki/MiniDisc "MiniDisc"), and digital albums, as they were introduced.[[7]](#cite_note-About_Vinyl_Records-7) As part of a trend of shifting sales in the [music industry](/wiki/Music_industry "Music industry"), some observers feel that the early 21st century experienced the [death of the album](/wiki/Death_of_the_album "Death of the album").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Length

An album may contain any number of tracks. In the United States, [The Recording Academy](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy")'s rules for [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") state that an album must comprise a minimum total playing time of 15 minutes with at least five distinct tracks or a minimum total playing time of 30 minutes with no minimum track requirement.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In the United Kingdom, the criteria for the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart") is that a recording counts as an "album" if it either has more than four tracks or lasts more than 25 minutes.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Sometimes shorter albums are referred to as [mini-albums](/wiki/Mini-album "Mini-album") or [EPs](/wiki/EPs "EPs").[[18]](#cite_note-As_albums_fade_away,_music_industry_looks_to_shorter_records-18) Albums such as *[Tubular Bells](/wiki/Tubular_Bells "Tubular Bells")*, *[Amarok](/wiki/Amarok_(Mike_Oldfield_album) "Amarok (Mike Oldfield album)")*, and *[Hergest Ridge](/wiki/Hergest_Ridge_(album) "Hergest Ridge (album)")* by [Mike Oldfield](/wiki/Mike_Oldfield "Mike Oldfield"), and [Yes's](/wiki/Yes_(band) "Yes (band)") *[Close to the Edge](/wiki/Close_to_the_Edge "Close to the Edge")*, include fewer than four tracks, but still surpass the 25-minute mark. The album *[Dopesmoker](/wiki/Dopesmoker "Dopesmoker")* by [Sleep](/wiki/Sleep_(band) "Sleep (band)") contains only a single track, but the composition is over 63 minutes long. There are no formal rules against [artists](/wiki/Artist "Artist") such as [Pinhead Gunpowder](/wiki/Pinhead_Gunpowder "Pinhead Gunpowder") referring to their own releases under thirty minutes as "albums".

If an album becomes too long to fit onto a single vinyl record or CD, it may be released as a [double album](/wiki/Double_album "Double album") where two vinyl [LPs](/wiki/LPs "LPs") or compact discs are packaged together in a single case, or a [triple album](/wiki/Triple_album "Triple album") containing three LPs or compact discs. Recording artists who have an extensive back catalogue may re-release several CDs in one single box with a unified design, often containing one or more albums (in this scenario, these releases can sometimes be referred to as a "two (or three)-fer"), or a compilation of previously unreleased recordings. These are known as [box sets](/wiki/Box_set "Box set"). Some musical artists have also released more than three compact discs or LP records of new recordings at once, in the form of boxed sets, although in that case the work is still usually considered to be an album.

## Tracks

Main article: [Track listing](/wiki/Track_listing "Track listing")

Material (music or sounds) is stored on an album in sections termed tracks. A music track (often simply referred to as a track) is an individual [song](/wiki/Song "Song") or [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental "Instrumental") recording. The term is particularly associated with [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") where separate tracks are known as album tracks; the term is also used for other formats such as [EPs](/wiki/EP_(format) "EP (format)") and [singles](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)"). When vinyl records were the primary medium for audio recordings a track could be identified visually from the grooves and many album covers or sleeves included numbers for the tracks on each side. On a [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") the track number is indexed so that a player can jump straight to the start of any track. On digital music stores such as [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes") the term song is often used interchangeably with track regardless of whether there is any vocal content.

A track that has the same name as the album is called the title track. In the Korean music industry, the title track is used to refer to any song that has been promoted, such as a single, regardless of its title.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Bonus tracks

"Bonus track" redirects here. For the 2023 film, see [Bonus Track (film)](/wiki/Bonus_Track_(film) "Bonus Track (film)").

A **bonus track** (also known as a **bonus cut** or **bonus**) is a piece of music which has been included as an extra. This may be done as a marketing promotion or for other reasons. It is not uncommon to include singles, [B-sides](/wiki/B-side "B-side"), [live recordings](/wiki/Live_recording "Live recording"), and [demo recordings](/wiki/Demo_recording "Demo recording") as bonus tracks on reissues of old albums, where those tracks were not originally included. Online music stores allow buyers to create their own albums by selecting songs themselves[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]; bonus tracks may be included if a customer buys a whole album rather than just one or two songs from the artist. The song is not necessarily free, nor is it available as a stand-alone download, adding to the incentive to buy the complete album[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. In contrast to [hidden tracks](/wiki/Hidden_track "Hidden track"), bonus tracks are included on track listings and usually do not have a gap of silence between other album tracks. Bonus tracks on CD or vinyl albums are common in Japan for releases by European and North American artists; since importing international copies of the album can be cheaper than buying a domestically released version, Japanese releases often feature bonus tracks to incentivize domestic purchase.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Audio formats

See also: [Timeline of audio formats](/wiki/Timeline_of_audio_formats "Timeline of audio formats")

### Non-audio printed format

Main article: [Sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music")

Commercial sheet music is published in conjunction with the release of a new album (studio, compilation, soundtrack, etc.). A matching folio songbook is a compilation of the [music notation](/wiki/Music_notation "Music notation") of all the songs included in that particular album. It typically has the album's artwork on its cover and, in addition to sheet music, it includes photos of the artist.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Most pop and rock releases come in standard Piano/Vocal/Guitar notation format (and occasionally Easy Piano / E-Z Play Today).[[21]](#cite_note-21) Rock-oriented releases may also come in Guitar Recorded Versions edition, which are note-for-note transcriptions written directly from artist recordings.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Vinyl records

Main article: [LP record](/wiki/LP_record "LP record")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Record-Album-02.jpg/250px-Record-Album-02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Record-Album-02.jpg)

A vinyl LP on a turntable

Vinyl [LP records](/wiki/LP_records "LP records") have two sides, each comprising one-half of the album. If a [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") or [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") album contained tracks released separately as commercial [singles](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)"), they were conventionally placed in particular positions on the album.[[7]](#cite_note-About_Vinyl_Records-7) During the sixties, particularly in the UK, singles were generally released separately from albums. Today, many commercial albums of music tracks feature one or more singles, which are released separately to radio, TV or the Internet as a way of promoting the album.[[23]](#cite_note-Chronology:_Technology_and_the_Music_Industry-23) Albums have been issued that are compilations of older tracks not originally released together, such as singles not originally found on albums, [A-sides](/wiki/A-sides "A-sides") of singles, or unfinished "[demo](/wiki/Demo_(music) "Demo (music)")" recordings.[[7]](#cite_note-About_Vinyl_Records-7)

Double albums during the seventies were sometimes sequenced for [record changers](/wiki/Record_changers "Record changers"). In the case of a two-record set, for example, sides 1 and 4 would be stamped on one record, and sides 2 and 3 on the other. The user would stack the two records onto the spindle of an automatic record changer, with side 1 on the bottom and side 2 (on the other record) on top. Side 1 would automatically drop onto the [turntable](/wiki/Turntable "Turntable") and be played. When finished, the tone arm's position would trigger a mechanism which moved the arm out of the way, dropped the record with side 2, and played it. When both records had been played, the user would pick up the stack, turn it over, and put them back on the spindle—sides 3 and 4 would then play in sequence.[[7]](#cite_note-About_Vinyl_Records-7) Record changers were used for many years of the LP era, but eventually fell out of use.

### 8-track tape

Main article: [8-track cartridge](/wiki/8-track_cartridge "8-track cartridge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Leitor_de_cartuchos_JVC.jpg/250px-Leitor_de_cartuchos_JVC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leitor_de_cartuchos_JVC.jpg)

A typical 8-track tape player

8-track tape (formally Stereo 8: commonly known as the eight-track cartridge, eight-track tape, or simply eight-track) is a [magnetic tape sound recording](/wiki/Magnetic_tape_sound_recording "Magnetic tape sound recording") technology popular in the United States[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24) from the mid-1960s to the late 1970s when the [Compact Cassette](/wiki/Compact_Cassette "Compact Cassette") format took over.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24)[[25]](#cite_note-:1-25) The format is regarded as an obsolete technology, and was relatively unknown outside the United States, the United Kingdom, Canada and Australia.[[25]](#cite_note-:1-25)[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24)

Stereo 8 was created in 1964 by a consortium led by [Bill Lear](/wiki/Bill_Lear "Bill Lear") of [Lear Jet Corporation](/wiki/Lear_Jet_Corporation "Lear Jet Corporation"), along with [Ampex](/wiki/Ampex "Ampex"), [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"), [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors"), [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola"), and [RCA Victor Records](/wiki/RCA_Victor_Records "RCA Victor Records"). It was a further development of the similar [Stereo-Pak](/wiki/Stereo-Pak "Stereo-Pak") four-track cartridge created by [Earl "Madman" Muntz](/wiki/Earl_%22Madman%22_Muntz "Earl \"Madman\" Muntz"). A later [quadraphonic](/wiki/Quadraphonic "Quadraphonic") version of the format was announced by RCA in April 1970 and first known as Quad-8, then later changed to just Q8.

### Compact cassette

Main article: [Compact Cassette](/wiki/Compact_Cassette "Compact Cassette")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Compact_audio_cassette_2.jpg/250px-Compact_audio_cassette_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Compact_audio_cassette_2.jpg)

A blank compact cassette tape and case

The Compact Cassette was a popular medium for distributing pre-recorded music from the early 1970s to the early 2000s.[[26]](#cite_note-Daniel-26) The first "Compact Cassette" was introduced by [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips") in August 1963 in the form of a prototype.[[27]](#cite_note-History_of_Compact_Cassette-27) Compact Cassettes became especially popular during the 1980s after the advent of the Sony [Walkman](/wiki/Walkman "Walkman"), which allowed the person to control what they listened to.[[27]](#cite_note-History_of_Compact_Cassette-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The Walkman was convenient because of its size, the device could fit in most pockets and often came equipped with a clip for belts or pants.[[27]](#cite_note-History_of_Compact_Cassette-27)

The compact cassette used double-sided [magnetic tape](/wiki/Magnetic_tape "Magnetic tape") to distribute music for commercial sale.[[27]](#cite_note-History_of_Compact_Cassette-27)[[29]](#cite_note-The_History_of_Recorded_Music-29) The music is recorded on both the "A" and "B" side of the tape, with cassette being "turned" to play the other side of the album.[[27]](#cite_note-History_of_Compact_Cassette-27) Compact Cassettes were also a popular way for musicians to record "[Demos](/wiki/Demo_(music) "Demo (music)")" or "Demo Tapes" of their music to distribute to various record labels, in the hopes of acquiring a [recording contract](/wiki/Recording_contract "Recording contract").[[30]](#cite_note-The_Death_of_the_Demo_Tape_(At_Last)-30)

Compact cassettes also saw the creation of [mixtapes](/wiki/Mixtape "Mixtape"), which are tapes containing a compilation of songs created by any average listener of music.[[31]](#cite_note-Mixtape_History-31) The songs on a mixtape generally relate to one another in some way, whether it be a conceptual theme or an overall sound.[[31]](#cite_note-Mixtape_History-31) After the introduction of Compact discs, the term "Mixtape" began to apply to any personal compilation of songs on any given format.[[31]](#cite_note-Mixtape_History-31)

The sales of Compact Cassettes eventually began to decline in the 1990s, after the release and distribution [Compact Discs](/wiki/Compact_Disc "Compact Disc"). The 2010s saw a revival of Compact Cassettes by [independent record labels](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label") and DIY musicians who preferred the format because of its difficulty to [share over the Internet](/wiki/Online_piracy "Online piracy").[[32]](#cite_note-Cassette_Revival-32)

### Compact disc

Main article: [Compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Led_Zeppelin_III_by_Led_Zeppelin_%28Vinyl-1970%29.png/250px-Led_Zeppelin_III_by_Led_Zeppelin_%28Vinyl-1970%29.png)](/wiki/File:Led_Zeppelin_III_by_Led_Zeppelin_(Vinyl-1970).png)

The ten-track [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") studio album *[Led Zeppelin III](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin_III "Led Zeppelin III")*

The compact disc format replaced both the vinyl record and the cassette as the standard for the commercial mass-market distribution of physical music albums.[[33]](#cite_note-The_history_of_the_CD_-_The_beginning-33) After the introduction of [music downloading](/wiki/Music_download "Music download") and MP3 players such as the [iPod](/wiki/IPod "IPod"), US [album sales](/wiki/Album_sales "Album sales") dropped 54.6% from 2001 to 2009.[[34]](#cite_note-Us_Album_Sales-34) The CD is a digital [data storage device](/wiki/Data_storage_device "Data storage device") which permits [digital recording](/wiki/Digital_recording "Digital recording") technology to be used to record and play-back the recorded music.[[29]](#cite_note-The_History_of_Recorded_Music-29)[[33]](#cite_note-The_history_of_the_CD_-_The_beginning-33)

### MP3 albums, and similar

Main article: [Music download](/wiki/Music_download "Music download")

Most recently, the [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3") audio format has matured, revolutionizing the concept of digital storage. Early MP3 albums were essentially CD-rips created by early CD-[ripping](/wiki/Ripping "Ripping") software, and sometimes real-time rips from cassettes and vinyl.

The so-called "MP3 album" is not necessarily just in MP3 file format, in which higher quality formats such as [FLAC](/wiki/FLAC "FLAC") and [WAV](/wiki/WAV "WAV") can be used on storage media that MP3 albums reside on, such as [CD-R-ROMs](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM"), [hard drives](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive"), [flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory") (e.g. [thumbdrives](/wiki/Thumbdrives "Thumbdrives"), [MP3 players](/wiki/MP3_player "MP3 player"), [SD cards](/wiki/SD_card "SD card")), etc.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Types of album

See also: [Category:Album types](/wiki/Category:Album_types "Category:Album types")

The contents of the album are usually recorded in a [studio](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio") or live in concert, though may be recorded in other locations, such as at home (as with JJ Cale's *[Okie](/wiki/Okie_(J._J._Cale_album) "Okie (J. J. Cale album)")*,[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) Beck's *[Odelay](/wiki/Odelay "Odelay")*,[[37]](#cite_note-37) David Gray's *[White Ladder](/wiki/White_Ladder "White Ladder")*,[[38]](#cite_note-38) and others),[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) in the field—as with early blues recordings,[[42]](#cite_note-42) in prison,[[43]](#cite_note-43) or with a mobile recording unit such as the [Rolling Stones Mobile Studio](/wiki/Rolling_Stones_Mobile_Studio "Rolling Stones Mobile Studio").[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Studio

"Studio album" redirects here. For the album by Tages, see [*Studio* (album)](/wiki/Studio_(album) "Studio (album)").

Most albums are **studio albums**—that is, they are recorded in a [recording studio](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio") with equipment meant to give those overseeing the recording as much control as possible over the sound of the album. They minimize external noises and reverberations and have highly sensitive microphones and [sound mixing](/wiki/Sound_mixing "Sound mixing") equipment. Band members may record their parts in separate rooms or at separate times, listening to the other parts of the track with headphones to keep the timing right. In the 2000s, with the advent of [digital recording](/wiki/Digital_recording "Digital recording"), it became possible for musicians to record their part of a song in another studio in another part of the world, and send their contribution digitally to be included in the final product.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Live

"Live album" redirects here. For other uses, see [Live album (disambiguation)](/wiki/Live_album_(disambiguation) "Live album (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Artie_Kane_at_20th_Century_Fox.jpg/250px-Artie_Kane_at_20th_Century_Fox.jpg)](/wiki/File:Artie_Kane_at_20th_Century_Fox.jpg)

An orchestra recorded "live" in the studio

Recordings that are done in one, 'straight-through' take without [overdubbing](/wiki/Overdubbing "Overdubbing") or [multi-tracking](/wiki/Multitrack_recording "Multitrack recording") are termed "live",[[46]](#cite_note-46) even when done in a studio.[[47]](#cite_note-47) However, the common understanding of a **live album** is one that was recorded at a concert with a public audience,[[48]](#cite_note-48) even when the recording is overdubbed or multi-tracked.[[49]](#cite_note-49) To help differentiate between the two categories, the descriptor "live-to-tape" is often used

Concert or stage performances are recorded using [remote recording](/wiki/Remote_recording "Remote recording") techniques. Albums may be recorded at a single [concert](/wiki/Concert "Concert"), or combine recordings made at multiple concerts. They may include applause, laughter and other noise from the audience, comments by the performers between pieces, improvisation, and so on. They may use [multitrack recording](/wiki/Multitrack_recording "Multitrack recording") direct from the stage sound system (rather than microphones placed among the audience), and can employ additional manipulation and effects during post-production to enhance the quality of the recording.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Notable early live albums include the double album of [Benny Goodman](/wiki/Benny_Goodman "Benny Goodman"), *[The Famous 1938 Carnegie Hall Jazz Concert](/wiki/The_Famous_1938_Carnegie_Hall_Jazz_Concert "The Famous 1938 Carnegie Hall Jazz Concert")*, released in 1950.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Live [double albums](/wiki/Double_album "Double album") later became popular during the 1970s. Appraising the concept in *[Christgau's Record Guide: Rock Albums of the Seventies](/wiki/Christgau%27s_Record_Guide:_Rock_Albums_of_the_Seventies "Christgau's Record Guide: Rock Albums of the Seventies")* (1981), [Robert Christgau](/wiki/Robert_Christgau "Robert Christgau") said most "are profit-taking recaps marred by sound and format inappropriate to phonographic reproduction (you can't put sights, smells, or fellowship on audio tape). But for [Joe Cocker](/wiki/Joe_Cocker "Joe Cocker") and [Bette Midler](/wiki/Bette_Midler "Bette Midler") and [Bob-Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan")-in-the-arena, the form makes a compelling kind of sense."[[51]](#cite_note-CG-51)

Among the best selling live albums are [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton")'s *[Unplugged](/wiki/Unplugged_(Eric_Clapton_album) "Unplugged (Eric Clapton album)")* (1992), selling over 26 million copies,[[52]](#cite_note-52) [Garth Brooks](/wiki/Garth_Brooks "Garth Brooks")' *[Double Live](/wiki/Double_Live_(Garth_Brooks_album) "Double Live (Garth Brooks album)")* (1998), over 21 million copies,[[53]](#cite_note-53) and [Peter Frampton](/wiki/Peter_Frampton "Peter Frampton")'s *[Frampton Comes Alive!](/wiki/Frampton_Comes_Alive! "Frampton Comes Alive!")* (1976), over 11 million copies.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Solo

For albums titled "Solo", see [Solo (disambiguation) § Albums](/wiki/Solo_(disambiguation)#Albums "Solo (disambiguation)").

A **solo album**, in [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music"), is an album recorded by a current or former member of a [musical group](/wiki/Musical_group "Musical group") which is released under that artist's name only, even though some or all other band members may be involved. The solo album appeared as early as the late 1940s. A 1947 *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine article heralded "[Margaret Whiting](/wiki/Margaret_Whiting "Margaret Whiting") huddling with [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") execs over her first solo album on which she will be backed by [Frank De Vol](/wiki/Frank_De_Vol "Frank De Vol")".[[55]](#cite_note-55) There is no formal definition setting forth the amount of participation a band member can solicit from other members of their band, and still have the album referred to as a solo album. One reviewer wrote that [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr")'s third venture, *[Ringo](/wiki/Ringo_(album) "Ringo (album)")*, "[t]echnically... wasn't a solo album because all four [Beatles](/wiki/Beatles "Beatles") appeared on it".[[56]](#cite_note-56) Three of the four members of the Beatles released solo albums while the group was officially still together.

A performer may record a solo album for several reasons. A [solo performer](/wiki/Solo_(music) "Solo (music)") working with other members will typically have full creative control of the band, be able to hire and fire accompanists, and get the majority of the proceeds.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The performer may be able to produce songs that differ widely from the sound of the band with which the performer has been associated, or that the group as a whole chose not to include in its own albums. [Graham Nash](/wiki/Graham_Nash "Graham Nash") of [the Hollies](/wiki/The_Hollies "The Hollies") described his experience in developing a solo album as follows: "The thing that I go through that results in a solo album is an interesting process of collecting songs that can't be done, for whatever reason, by a lot of people".[[57]](#cite_note-57) A solo album may also represent the departure of the performer from the group.

### Compilation album

Main article: [Compilation album](/wiki/Compilation_album "Compilation album")

A [compilation album](/wiki/Compilation_album "Compilation album") is a collection of material from various recording projects or various artists, assembled with a theme such as the "greatest hits" from one artist, B-sides and rarities by one artist, or selections from a [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label"), a [musical genre](/wiki/Musical_genre "Musical genre"), a certain time period, or a regional music scene. Promotional [sampler albums](/wiki/Sampler_album "Sampler album") are compilations.

#### Tribute or cover

Further information: [List of tribute albums](/wiki/List_of_tribute_albums "List of tribute albums")

See also: [Category:Covers albums](/wiki/Category:Covers_albums "Category:Covers albums")

"Cover album" redirects here. not to be confused with [Album cover](/wiki/Album_cover "Album cover"). For the misono album, see [Cover Album (album)](/wiki/Cover_Album_(album) "Cover Album (album)").

A tribute or **cover album** is a compilation of [cover versions](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") of songs or instrumental compositions. Its concept may involve various artists covering the songs of a single artist, genre or period, a single artist covering the songs of various artists or a single artist, genre or period, or any variation of an album of cover songs which is marketed as a "tribute".[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### Posthumous album

Further information: [List of music released posthumously](/wiki/List_of_music_released_posthumously "List of music released posthumously")

See also: [Category:Albums published posthumously](/wiki/Category:Albums_published_posthumously "Category:Albums published posthumously")

"Posthumous album" redirects here; not to be confused with [Posthumous (album)](/wiki/Posthumous_(album) "Posthumous (album)").

A **posthumous album** is an album released after an artist's [death](/wiki/Death "Death"). These albums are often compilation albums, which sometimes feature unreleased music by the deceased artists. However, posthumous albums can also be studio albums, live albums or solo albums that primarily contain previously unreleased material.

Album releases that are posthumous which include new music often have mixed reviews (i.e., aside from *[Greatest Hits](/wiki/Greatest_Hits "Greatest Hits")* compilations). Opinions on the merits of releasing music by an artist posthumously vary at large, with some views expressing support to the idea that listeners would want to hear unpublished catalogues from an artist, in particular if the artist's estate and family are directly involved in assuring the artist's vision.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Opposite to this, other views reflect the idea that such releases are likely without consent by the artists who have passed, and as a product of this, posthumous releases could never meet the visions of artists themselves, thereby possibly negatively affecting the perceived [musicality](/wiki/Musicality "Musicality") and overall legacy of the artists.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Examples of successful posthumous album releases include [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)")'s 1994 first live album, *[MTV Unplugged in New York](/wiki/MTV_Unplugged_in_New_York "MTV Unplugged in New York")*, [the Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.")'s 1997 *[Life After Death](/wiki/Life_After_Death "Life After Death")* (released sixteen days after his death), and more recently, [Mac Miller](/wiki/Mac_Miller "Mac Miller")'s 2020 posthumous album, *[Circles](/wiki/Circles_(Mac_Miller_album) "Circles (Mac Miller album)")*.
